{{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}}, located in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土地理院}}''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''{{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery}} (Sueki pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器|1=Haji pottery}} (Haji pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Horse trappings|ja|馬具}}''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html Takakurayama Kofun – Ise City, Mie Prefecture]

{{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|12=draft}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}}, located in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}.
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=draft}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土地理院|12=draft}}''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=draft}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|12=draft}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|12=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|12=draft}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|12=draft}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=draft}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''{{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|12=draft}} (Sueki pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器|1=Haji pottery|12=draft}} (Haji pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Horse trappings|ja|馬具|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|12=draft}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|12=draft}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html Takakurayama Kofun – Ise City, Mie Prefecture]

revision.text:
[[Image:Takakura-yama(Ise)_01.JPG|thumb|300px|right|上空から見た高倉山]] '''高倉山'''（たかくらやま）は[[三重県]][[伊勢市]]の[[伊勢神宮]]外宮の敷地にある山域の総称。
==概要== [[Image:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|right|高倉山遠景]] 高倉山は[[豊受大神宮|伊勢神宮の外宮]]の宮域周辺の高佐山、日鷲山（ひわしやま）、賀利佐我峰、佐貫山、音無山などで構成される山域の総称であるが、標高117[[メートル|m]]で最高峰の日鷲山（{{ウィキ座標|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|_region:JA_|北緯34度28分55秒，東経136度41分57秒}}）を高倉山と呼ぶことが多く、[[国土地理院]]の地図上では日鷲山山頂が高倉山とされている。日鷲山の山頂には高倉山古墳という[[円墳]]があり、山域には他にも古墳が存在するという。古くは高賀佐山、高坐山と呼ばれていたが、坐を「くら」と誤って読まれたため「高倉山」になったとされる。
古くは[[神宮式年遷宮]]での用材を供給する[[御杣山]]（みそまやま）であったが、[[ヒノキ]]が枯渇したため御杣山は木曾谷へ落ち着くまで数度変遷している。
[[大正]]末期より[[神路山]]・[[島路山]]とともに高倉山でのヒノキの植林が行なわれ、[[2125年]]ころより遷宮に使えるようになるという。山林の保護のため、[[昭和]]50年代から高倉山への入山が禁止されている。
==高倉山古墳== {{see also|高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)}} [[Image:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|[[高倉山古墳 (伊勢市)|高倉山古墳]]]] [[古墳時代]]後期の[[円墳]]で、[[墳丘]]の径が32m、高さが8mだが、墳丘に見合わない全長18.5m、高さ4.1mの両袖型の[[横穴式石室]]を持つ。長さ9.7メートル、幅3.3メートル、高さ4.1メートルの玄室をもつ。鎌倉時代には盗掘されていたと考えられている。[[伊勢神宮|神宮]]の敷地内にあり、室町時代末期から江戸時代末期までは[[天岩戸]]として参拝の対象となったが、高倉山への入山禁止により見ることができなくなった。当初は[[7世紀]]に作られたと考えられていたが、[[1975年]]に行われた発掘調査の結果、[[6世紀]]中ごろに作られた古墳であると推測されている。この調査で[[須恵器]]、[[土師器]]、[[馬具]]、[[鉄刀]]、[[刀子]]、[[玉]]類などが出土した。
==関連項目== *[[伊勢神宮]] *[[日本の古墳一覧]]
==外部リンク== *[https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html 三重県伊勢市 高倉山古墳]
{{Japan-geo-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:たかくらやま}} [[Category:山岳名目録]] [[Category:伊勢神宮]] [[Category:三重県の山]] [[Category:伊勢市の地理]]

The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''{{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Sueki pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器|1=Haji pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Haji pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Horse trappings|ja|馬具|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

<!--Takakurayama (Ise)-->

{{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, located in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土地理院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''{{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Sueki pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器|1=Haji pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Haji pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Horse trappings|ja|馬具|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html Takakurayama Kofun – Ise City, Mie Prefecture]

The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''{{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sue pottery|13=User:Immanuelle/Sue pottery|qq=}} (Sueki pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器|1=Haji pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji pottery|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji pottery|qq=}} (Haji pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Horse trappings|ja|馬具|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horse trappings|qq=}}''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Shikinen Sengu]] → [[Jingū Shikinen Sengū]]
revision.text:
{{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}}, located in {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise, Mie|qq=}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefecture|qq=}}.
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|ja|国土地理院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|qq=}}''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|qq=}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''{{ill|Sue pottery|ja|須恵器|1=Sue pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sue pottery|13=User:Immanuelle/Sue pottery|qq=}} (Sueki pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器|1=Haji pottery|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji pottery|13=User:Immanuelle/Haji pottery|qq=}} (Haji pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Horse trappings|ja|馬具|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horse trappings|qq=}}''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html Takakurayama Kofun – Ise City, Mie Prefecture]

revision.text:
{{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}}, located in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|qq=}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html Takakurayama Kofun – Ise City, Mie Prefecture]
{{Japan-geo-stub}}

}}

The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|qq=}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|神宮式年遷宮|" to "|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|"
revision.text:
{{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}}, located in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|qq=}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html Takakurayama Kofun – Ise City, Mie Prefecture]

At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|qq=}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html Takakurayama Kofun – Ise City, Mie Prefecture]

{{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, located in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|qq=}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''

revision.comment: attempting to just clean out the mojibake interwikis
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, located in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|qq=}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html Takakurayama Kofun – Ise City, Mie Prefecture]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11668768]] Wikidata name: Mount Takakura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, located in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|qq=}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|qq=}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html Takakurayama Kofun – Ise City, Mie Prefecture]

Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|qq=|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|qq=|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|qq=|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11668768]] Wikidata name: Mount Takakura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Add wikidata]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, located in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyouke Daijingu|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Daijingu|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|qq=|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|qq=|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|qq=|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.

Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''

<!--Takakurayama (Ise)--> [[Category:Geography of Ise, Mie]] [[Category:Ise Shrine]] [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] [[en:Takakurayama (Ise)]]<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11668768]] Wikidata name: Mount Takakura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, located in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|qq=|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|qq=|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|qq=|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11668768]] Wikidata name: Mount Takakura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, located in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|qq=|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|qq=|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|qq=|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html Takakurayama Kofun – Ise City, Mie Prefecture]

== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
