<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11668768]] Wikidata name: Mount Takakura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, located in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html Takakurayama Kofun – Ise City, Mie Prefecture]

== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11668768]] Wikidata name: Mount Takakura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, located in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11668768]] Wikidata name: Mount Takakura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]]'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, located in [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''[[:en:Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''[[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] (Sueki pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] (Haji pottery)''' * '''[[:en:Horse tack|Horse trappings]]''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html Takakurayama Kofun – Ise City, Mie Prefecture]

However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|en|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|WD=Q2986578|lt=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan}}''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''{{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} (Sueki pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} (Haji pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Horse tack|en|Horse tack|WD=Q950799|lt=Horse trappings}}''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|en|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|WD=Q2986578|lt=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan}}''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''{{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} (Sueki pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} (Haji pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Horse tack|en|Horse tack|WD=Q950799|lt=Horse trappings}}''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''

These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''{{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} (Sueki pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} (Haji pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Horse tack|en|Horse tack|WD=Q950799|lt=Horse trappings}}''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]]
{{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, located in {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}.
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|en|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|WD=Q2986578|lt=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan}}''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''{{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} (Sueki pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery}} (Haji pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Horse tack|en|Horse tack|WD=Q950799|lt=Horse trappings}}''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.ise.mie.jp/cul_spo_edu/culture/bunkazai_shiseki/bunkazai/kinenbutsu/1005935.html Takakurayama Kofun – Ise City, Mie Prefecture]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高倉山 (伊勢市)]][[lld:Mont Takakura]]
{{ill|Takakurayama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama (Ise)|qq=}} (たかくらやま) is a collective name for the mountainous area within the precincts of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}'s {{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, located in {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie|1=Ise, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}.
== Overview == {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_02.JPG|thumb|256px|Distant view of Takakurayama]]}} Takakurayama is a general term for the mountainous area surrounding '''{{ill|Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekū (Ise Grand Shrine)|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''', consisting of '''Takasayama''', '''Hiwashiyama (日鷲山)''', '''Karisaka-mine (賀利佐我峰)''', '''Sanukiyama (佐貫山)''', and '''Otonashiyama (音無山)'''.
However, the '''highest peak is Hiwashiyama, standing at 117 meters''' ({{coord|34|28|55|N|136|41|57|E|region:JP|display=inline}}), which is '''commonly referred to as Takakurayama'''. On maps by the '''{{ill|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|en|Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|WD=Q2986578|lt=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan|1=Geospatial Information Authority of Japan}}''', the peak of Hiwashiyama is labeled as '''Takakurayama'''.
At the summit of Hiwashiyama, there is an ancient Kofun known as '''{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}''', a '''circular Kofun (円墳, enpun)'''. Other ancient Kofuns are also believed to exist within the area. Historically, the mountain was called '''Takakasayama''' or '''Takazasayama''', but due to a misreading of "坐" as "Kura," it became known as '''Takakurayama'''.
Takakurayama was historically designated as a '''sacred forest (御杣山, Misomayama)''', supplying timber for the '''{{ill|Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|lt=Shikinen Sengu|ja|神宮式年遷宮|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|1=Jingū Shikinen Sengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinen Sengu|qq=}}''' rebuilding rituals. However, due to the depletion of '''Japanese cypress (ヒノキ, Hinoki)''', the '''official timber source changed multiple times''' until finally settling in the '''Kiso Valley'''.
Since the late '''Taishō period''', '''reforestation projects''' began on '''Takakurayama''', along with '''{{ill|Kaminomichi-yama|ja|神路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminomichi-yama|1=Mount Kamiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590896}}''' and '''{{ill|Shimonomichi-yama|ja|島路山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimonomichi-yama|1=Mount Shimaji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477059}}'''. These forests are expected to be mature enough for '''use in the shrine rebuilding ceremonies by the year 2125'''. To protect the forest, '''entry to Takakurayama has been prohibited since the late Shōwa era (1970s)'''.
== Takakurayama Kofun == {{see also|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳_(伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}}} {{right|[[File:Takakura-yama(Ise)_03.JPG|thumb|250px|{{ill|Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|ja|高倉山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakurayama Kofun (Ise)|qq=}}]]}} Takakurayama Kofun is a '''late Kofun period circular Kofun''' (円墳, enpun) with a '''diameter of 32 meters and a height of 8 meters'''. However, it features an '''unusually large stone chamber (横穴式石室, yokoana-shiki sekishitsu)''', measuring '''18.5 meters in total length and 4.1 meters in height''', which is disproportionate to the size of the mound.
The '''burial chamber (玄室, genshitsu)''' itself is '''9.7 meters long, 3.3 meters wide, and 4.1 meters high'''. Evidence suggests it was '''looted during the Kamakura period'''.
Since the mound is located '''within the precincts of Ise Grand Shrine''', it was '''worshipped as Ama-no-Iwato (天岩戸, the "Heavenly Rock Cave")''' from the '''late Muromachi to the late Edo period'''. However, due to the '''ban on entry to Takakurayama''', it is no longer accessible to visitors.
Initially, the kofun was believed to have been built in the '''7th century''', but '''excavations in 1975''' revealed that it was '''constructed in the mid-6th century'''. The excavation uncovered artifacts such as: * '''{{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} (Sueki pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji pottery|1=Haji pottery}} (Haji pottery)''' * '''{{ill|Horse tack|en|Horse tack|WD=Q950799|lt=Horse trappings|1=Horse tack}}''' * '''{{ill|Tachi|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachi|qq=}} (Iron swords)''' * '''{{ill|Kozuka|ja|刀子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kozuka|qq=}} (Small knives)''' * '''Various beads (玉類, gyokurui)'''
